Farn Polrucal PnacrrcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
dugust 16,2OO5
Richard G. Barakat
700 Braewood Drive
Bradbury, CA 91010
Re: Your Request Regarding Our File No. I-04-209
Dear Mr. Barakat:
In response to your question in our telephone conversation today, our records do
not reflect any changes to the above referenced letter issued to you on Novemb er 19,
2004.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
el
By:
w
Counsel,
Division
WJL:rd


Re:
Farn PolrrrcAt- PRACTTcES CouurssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5ffi0 e Fax (916) 322-0886
November 19,20A4
Richard G. Barakat
700 Braewood Drive
Bradbury, CA 91010
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. l-04-209
Dear Mr. Barakat:
This letter is in response to your request for informal assistancel regarding the
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the *Act').z This letter should not be construed
as assistance on any conduct that may have already taken place. In addition, this letter is
based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (,Commission")
does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FppC
Ops. 71; Govt. Code section 83114.)
QUESTTONS
l. Do you have a conflict of interest under the Act in a decision regarding the
annexation of a retirement home which is located within 500 feet of yourhome?
2. May you send a letter in your official capacity to your constituents informing
thern when there is an issue on which you cannot represent them?
CONCLUSIONS
l. You will have a conflict of interest under the Act unless you can establish that
the annexation of the property will have no material financial affecton your home.
Additionally, you may be able to participate in the decision under the "public generally',
exception.
I Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 14; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.j 
-
' Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise indicated. All regulatory references are to Title 2 of the Code of Regulation unless
otherwise indicated.
:


File No. I-04-209
Page No. 2
2. Yes. An official is not attempting to use his or herposition to influence a
goveflrmental decision when he or she communicates with the general public or the press.
FACTS
In our recent telephone conversation, you provided information that you are an
elected city council member for the City of Bradbury. The city is composed entirely of
single-family homes and no businesses. There are approximately 300 homes and 1,000
residents in the city. In your district, there are less than 100 homes and approximately
200 residents. Your letter concerns two issues.
ISSUE l: You own a single-family residence within 500 feet of a retirernent
home. The city is considering annexation of that property. You indicated that the
retirement home is already developd and currently uses a Bradbury address, including
zip code, in its mailing address. You have also indicated that you have two "letters from
real estate brokers stating that the annexation would not have an effect qp or down on the
value of the adjacent properties." In our telephone conversation, you indicated that there
are eight or nine other homes in your district that are within 500 feet of the retirement
home. In all, this represents more than ten percent of the homes in your district.
ISSUE 2: You would like to send a letter, in your official capacityand on your
official letterhead, to your constituents informing them when there is an issue on which
you cannot represent them, inviting them to come and express their opinion on the issue.
This mailing would be done at your own e*perrse.3
ANALYSIS
Porunrrn r, Coxrr,rcr oF INTEREsT
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 810016).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
othenvise using his or her official position to influence a govenrmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualiffing conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700.) The geireral rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a
governme,ntal decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one
or more of his or her financial interests.
3 Sit"" the mass mailing provisions of the Act only apply when the costs ofdistribution are paid
forwithpublicmonies(seeregulation 18901(a[3)(A),andyouhaveindicatedthatyouwillpayfortliese
mailings at your own expense, this issue is not analyzed under the mass mailing provisions oithe Act but,
rather, as an attempt to influence a governmental decision under the Act's confliit of interest provisions.


File No. I-04-209
Page No. 3
Srnps 1 lxn 2
Anr You A Purr,rc orrrcnr,? Anr vou M,lrcNc, pantrcrplrnvq oR rxrt uexcriyc
,r GovnnxMENTAL Drcrsrorv?
As a Bradbury city council membeq you are a public official under the Act (section
8244q. Consequently, you may not make, participate in making or othe6rrise use your
official position to influence any decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable *ut".iul
financial effect on any economic interest you mayhave.
As a member of the Bradbury City Council, since you will be called upon to
participate in a governmental decision regarding the annexation of the referenced property,
you would be making, participating in making or otherwise using your officiat position to-
influence a govemmental decision.
Additionally, you have indicated that you wish to send a letter to your constituents,
in yonr official capacrty, informing them when there is an issue on which you cannot
represent them and inviting thern to come and express theiropinion on the issue. For
putposes of determining when an official is using or atternpting to use his or her position to
influence a governmental decision regulation l970z3(a) provides:
*(a) With regard to a governmental decision which is
within or before an official's agency or an agency appointed by or
subject to the budgetary control of his or her agency, the official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision i{ for the purposes of influencing the decision, the official
contacts, or appears beforg or otherwise attempts to influence, any
member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency. ...,,
(emphasis added.)
However, regulation 187A2.4{b)(2) provides that notwithstanding the provisions
of regulation 187023@), the official is not attempting to use his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision if the official "communicates with the general public
or the press."
AccordinglY, nY contact you make in your official capacity with your constifuents
is not an attempt to influence a govemmental decision under the Act's conflict of interest
provisions
Srm 3
Porrr.rrrAr,r,y DrseuALrryING EcoNoMrc Irvrnnrsr
Section 87103 and regulation 18703 define economic interest to include business
entities, real property, sources of income, gifts, and personal finances. Based upon the
information you have provided, you have an economic interest in the real property which


File No. l-04-209
Page No. 4
is your residence. (section 87103O), regulation ls7a3.2(a).) Thus, we limit our
discussion to this economic interest.
Srnp4
Is TUB EcoNoivrrc Ixrrnssr Drnrcrr,y m.INDTRECTLy Irwor-vso rN TEE
GovrnxuENTAL Dncrsrov ?
In order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the
official's economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision. (Regulation 18704(a).) Forgovernmental decisions that affect
economic interests in real propertS the standards set forth in regulation 18704.2 apply:
Regulation I 87 0a.2@) states :
"(a) Real property in which apublic official has an economic
interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if any of
the following apply:
(l) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any
part of that real property, is located within 500 feet ofthe
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is
the subject of the governmental decision. ...
Since, :rs you have indicated, your property is within 500 feet of the property that
is the subject of the governmental decision, you have an economic interest that is directly
involved the governmental decision.
Srrr5
M,rrgnrnr,rrv Smr.lomn
Drnrcrr,y Iiwor,vno REAL hoprnrv TNTEREsT
Once the degree of involvernent is determined, Step 5 of the conflict-of-interest
analysis addresses the applicable materiality standard. Regulation 18705.2, subdivision
(aXl) sets forth the materiality standards for directly involved real property. It states:
"(a) Directly involved real property.
(1) Real property, other than leaseholds. The financial effect of a
governmental decision on the real propertyis presumed to be
material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not
reasonably foreseeable that the govemmemtal decision will have
any financial effect on the real property."
You have indicated that you have obtained information that there will be no
reasonably foreseeable financial efflect on any ofthe adjacent real property as a result of


FileNo. I-A4-2O9
Page No. 5
the governmental decision. This is a factual determination that you will have to make in
order to rebut the above presumption. If the presumption is rebutted by proof that it is
not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any financial effect
on your real property, not even a penny's worth, then the financial effect is deemed to be
not material, and the conflict-of-interest analysis would end at this step, as you would not
have a conflict of interest under the Act.
However, if the presumption is not rebutted and the financial effect is presumed to
be material, you would then move on to thenext steps of the analpis. again, this facfual
determination is one to be made by the official.
Srrp6
Rn lsoxa.rr,y Fonnsrranr,n
Once the applicable materially standmd has been applied, the next step is to
determine if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will meet the
applicable materiality standard for the economic interest involved. The effect of a
decision is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it
will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) If it is not reasonably foreseeable that there will be a
material financial effect on thepublic official's economic interes! he or she does not
have a conflict of interest within the meaning of the Act.
SrrpT
Punr,rc Gnxsnanr,y
Notwithstanding a determination that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material, a public
official does not have a disqualiSring conflict of interest in the governmental decision if
the governmental decision affects the public official's economic interest in a manner that
is indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally
(Regulation 18707(a).) The material financial effect of a governmental decislon on a
public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effects on thepublic
generally if both of the factors identified rmder regulation 18707.1, zubdivisions (b)(l)
and (bX2) are satisfied.
Srcxrrrcnxr Srcnmxr
Regulation 787A7 J, subdivision (bxl) provides that:
"The governmental decision will affect a 'significant segment, ofthepublic
generally if any of the following me affected as set forth below:"
fl...7
"(B) Real Property. For decisions that affect arealproperty
economic interest to a public official, the decision also affects


File No. I-04-209
Page No. 6
"(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all
homeowners in the jurisdiction of the official,s agency or the
district the official represents; or...." (Regulation
18707.1o)(lxB).)
SussrANTrALr,y rrrr S,c,Mr M.mvrn
The governmental decision will affect a public official's economic interest in
substantially the same manner as it will affect the significant segment identified above.
(Regulation I 87 07 .l (b)(2).)
AccordinglS if the govemmental decision will affect ten percent of the property
owners or homeowners in your district in substantially the same manner as it affecis your
real property, the public generally exception applies.
You have indicated that there are "eight or nine" houses within 500 feet of the
property which is the subject of the annexation decision, and that this represents over ten
percent of the "less than l0O homes" in your district. For the public generally exception
to apply, you will need to determine that at least ten percent ofthe homeovmers in your
district will be affected in substantially the same manner as yourresidence. This may
include homes located more than 500 feet from the property subject to the annexation
decision if they are affected in substantially the same manner and is again a factual
determination to be made by the official.
Srrp 8
Lnclr,r,y RrernRED PlRrrcrrlrrov
You have not presented ary facts suggesting that the final step, "legally required
participation" need be considered.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely
Luisa Menchaca
By:
Counsel,
Division
Enclosure
